modules.title: Module 1 · Python Foundations
modules.description: Build confidence with core Python through small, practical tasks.

lessons.title: Variables and data types
lessons.description: Store names, numbers and decisions in Python.

steps.title: Create useful variables
steps.content:
Variables are labelled containers. Python infers the type from the value.

Create name, age and is_learning variables, then print them.
steps.starter:
# Create the three variables below

# Print each variable
steps.hint: Use name = 'Anish', age = 45 and is_learning = True.
steps.solution:
name = 'Anish'
age = 45
is_learning = True
print(name)
print(age)
print(is_learning)

steps.title: Calculate a future age
steps.content:
Python can combine variables with arithmetic operators.

Create future_age by adding 5 to age, then print it.
steps.starter:
age = 45
# Create future_age and print it

steps.title: Choose the correct type
steps.content: Finish with a quick retrieval check.
steps.starter: print('Answer the quiz below.')

quiz.question: What is the type of the value '250'?

lessons.title: Lists and indexing
lessons.description: Work with ordered collections of values.

steps.title: Create and inspect a list
steps.content:
A list stores an ordered group of values.

Create sales = [120, 180, 90, 250] and print the first item.
steps.starter:
# Create the sales list

# Print the first item

steps.content: The built-in sum function adds numeric list values.
steps.starter:
sales = [120, 180, 90, 250]
# Print the total sales

steps.content: This list has four items, indexed from 0 to 3.
steps.starter:
sales = [120, 180, 90, 250]
print(sales[4])
steps.hint: The final valid index is 3, or use -1.

lessons.title: Conditions and decisions
lessons.description: Make programs respond differently to different values.

steps.title: Write an if statement
steps.content:
Conditions let a program choose what to do.

Print 'Target reached' when revenue is at least 1000.
steps.starter:
revenue = 1250

# Write the condition below
steps.hint: Use if revenue >= 1000: and indent the print line.

steps.title: Add an alternative
steps.content: An else block runs when the condition is false.
steps.starter:
revenue = 700
# Print 'Target reached' or 'Keep going'

steps.title: Read a condition
steps.content: Predict before running.
steps.starter: print('Complete the quiz below.')

quiz.question: What does 800 >= 1000 evaluate to?

lessons.title: Functions and reuse
lessons.description: Package logic into reusable building blocks.

steps.title: Define a function
steps.content:
A function gives a name to reusable logic.

Create calculate_total(values) that returns sum(values).
steps.starter:
# Define the function

print(calculate_total([10, 20, 30]))
steps.hint: Use def calculate_total(values): then return sum(values).

steps.title: Add a default argument
steps.content: Default values make parameters optional.
steps.starter:
def add_tax(amount, rate=0.2):
    # Return amount including tax
    pass

print(add_tax(100))

steps.title: Return, do not only print
steps.content: Printing displays a value. Returning sends it back to the caller.
steps.starter:
def double(number):
    print(number * 2)

result = double(5)
print('Result:', result)
steps.hint: Replace print inside the function with return.
steps.solution:
def double(number):
    return number * 2

result = double(5)
print('Result:', result)

modules.title: Module 2 · Data Analytics with pandas
modules.description: Move from Python syntax to realistic business-data analysis.

lessons.title: Create and inspect DataFrames
lessons.description: Turn records into a table and inspect its structure.

steps.title: Build a DataFrame

A DataFrame is pandas' table structure.

Create df, then print it without the row index so the table columns align cleanly.
steps.hint: Create df with pd.DataFrame(data), then use print(df.to_string(index=False)).

CSV is one of the most common data-exchange formats. pandas can read CSV text using StringIO.

Asha,North,1200
Ben,South,900
Cara,North,1500
'''

lessons.title: Merge related datasets
lessons.description: Combine customer and transaction tables using a shared key.

steps.title: Join two DataFrames
steps.content: Real analysis often requires combining datasets. merge joins tables using a shared key.
steps.starter:
import pandas as pd
customers = pd.DataFrame({'customer_id':[1,2,3], 'name':['Asha','Ben','Cara']})
orders = pd.DataFrame({'customer_id':[1,1,3], 'amount':[200,350,500]})

# Merge customers and orders on customer_id

steps.title: Keep customers without orders
steps.content: A left join keeps every row from the left table.
steps.starter: # Create all_customers using a left join

steps.content: Choose the join that preserves all rows from the first table.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: Think from the perspective of the left-hand DataFrame.

quiz.question: Which join keeps every customer even when they have no order?

lessons.title: Build pivot tables
lessons.description: Summarise multiple dimensions with compact analytical tables.

steps.title: Create a pivot table
steps.content: Pivot tables summarise values across categories.
steps.starter:
import pandas as pd
sales = pd.DataFrame({'region':['North','North','South','South'],'product':['A','B','A','B'],'revenue':[120,180,150,210]})
# Create pivot with region rows, product columns and revenue sums
steps.hint: Use pd.pivot_table with index='region', columns='product', values='revenue', aggfunc='sum'.
steps.solution:
pivot = pd.pivot_table(sales, index='region', columns='product', values='revenue', aggfunc='sum')
print(pivot)

steps.title: Add totals
steps.content: margins=True adds overall totals.
steps.starter: # Create pivot_with_totals with margins=True
steps.hint: Repeat the pivot_table call and add margins=True.
steps.solution:
pivot_with_totals = pd.pivot_table(sales, index='region', columns='product', values='revenue', aggfunc='sum', margins=True)
print(pivot_with_totals)

steps.title: Choose the purpose
steps.content: Recall what a pivot table is best at.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: What is the main purpose of a pivot table?

lessons.title: Create clear analytical charts
lessons.description: Turn pandas results into simple business visualisations.

steps.title: Create a bar chart
steps.content: Charts should answer a question clearly. pandas plotting provides a convenient starting point.
steps.starter:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
regional = pd.Series({'North':700, 'South':500, 'West':300})
# Create a bar chart and set the title to Regional Sales
steps.solution:
regional.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Regional Sales')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
print('Regional Sales')

steps.content: Axis labels help viewers interpret values.
steps.starter: # Add the y-axis label Revenue

modules.title: Module 3 · Web Scraping and APIs
modules.description: Learn how webpages, HTML and ethical data collection work before moving to live scraping.

lessons.title: Read HTML like data
lessons.description: Understand tags, attributes and the document tree.

steps.title: Find information in HTML
steps.content:
HTML describes the structure of a webpage. Tags such as h1, p and a identify different elements.

Use BeautifulSoup to extract the heading text from the sample HTML.

Life Sciences Jobs

Repeated requests can overload a website. A delay reduces request frequency.

Create a variable named delay_seconds with the value 2 and print it.

steps.content: Ethical scraping includes checking permissions and preferring official APIs where available.

modules.title: Module 4 · SQL with Python
modules.description: Query structured data with SQL and connect the results to pandas.

lessons.title: Query data with SELECT
lessons.description: Use SQL to retrieve columns and filter records.

steps.title: Run your first SQL query
steps.content:
SQLite is a lightweight database included with Python. A SELECT query retrieves data from a table.

Create the sample table, then select every row from sales.
steps.starter:
import sqlite3
import pandas as pd

conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
pd.DataFrame({
    'product':['A','B','C'],
    'region':['North','South','North'],
    'revenue':[120,180,150]
}).to_sql('sales', conn, index=False)

# Read all rows from sales into result
steps.solution:
result = pd.read_sql_query('SELECT * FROM sales', conn)
print(result.to_string(index=False))

steps.title: Filter rows with WHERE
steps.content:
WHERE keeps only rows matching a condition.

Return only rows where revenue is at least 150.
steps.starter: # Create high_revenue using a WHERE clause
steps.hint: Use SELECT * FROM sales WHERE revenue >= 150.
steps.solution:
high_revenue = pd.read_sql_query('SELECT * FROM sales WHERE revenue >= 150', conn)
print(high_revenue.to_string(index=False))

steps.title: Choose the correct clause
steps.content: Recall which SQL clause filters rows.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: Which SQL clause filters rows based on a condition?

lessons.title: Aggregate with SQL
lessons.description: Use GROUP BY, SUM and aliases to answer business questions.

steps.title: Calculate totals by region
steps.content: SQL aggregation works much like pandas groupby.
steps.starter:
import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
pd.DataFrame({
 'region':['North','South','North','West'],
 'revenue':[200,300,150,250]
}).to_sql('sales', conn, index=False)

# Query total revenue by region
steps.hint: Use SELECT region, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM sales GROUP BY region.
steps.solution:
regional = pd.read_sql_query('SELECT region, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM sales GROUP BY region', conn)
print(regional.to_string(index=False))

steps.title: Sort the summary
steps.content: ORDER BY controls the output order.
steps.starter: # Create ranked with highest total_revenue first
steps.solution:
ranked = pd.read_sql_query('SELECT region, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM sales GROUP BY region ORDER BY total_revenue DESC', conn)
print(ranked.to_string(index=False))

steps.title: Understand aliases
steps.content: An alias gives a calculated column a readable name.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: What does AS total_revenue do in SQL?

lessons.title: Join tables in SQL
lessons.description: Combine customer and order records using a shared identifier.

steps.title: Create an inner join
steps.content: A SQL join combines related rows from different tables.
steps.starter:
import sqlite3
import pandas as pd
conn = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
pd.DataFrame({'customer_id':[1,2,3], 'name':['Asha','Ben','Cara']}).to_sql('customers', conn, index=False)
pd.DataFrame({'customer_id':[1,1,3], 'amount':[200,350,500]}).to_sql('orders', conn, index=False)

# Join customers and orders on customer_id
steps.hint: Use INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id.
steps.solution:
joined = pd.read_sql_query('''
SELECT c.name, o.amount
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o
ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
''', conn)
print(joined.to_string(index=False))

steps.title: Keep unmatched customers
steps.content: A left join keeps every row from the left table.
steps.starter: # Create all_customers using LEFT JOIN
steps.hint: Replace INNER JOIN with LEFT JOIN.
steps.solution:
all_customers = pd.read_sql_query('''
SELECT c.name, o.amount
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o
ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
''', conn)
print(all_customers.to_string(index=False))

steps.title: Choose the right join
steps.content: Match the business requirement to the join type.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: Keep every customer from the first table.

quiz.question: Which join keeps customers who have no orders?

modules.title: Module 5 · Automation and APIs
modules.description: Work with JSON, simulated API responses and repeatable file-processing workflows.

lessons.title: Read and transform JSON
lessons.description: Convert JSON text into Python objects and extract nested values.

steps.title: Parse JSON text
steps.content:
APIs commonly return JSON. Python's json.loads() converts JSON text into dictionaries and lists.

Print the city from the nested office object.

steps.title: Check the status code
steps.content:
An API response should be checked before its data is used.

Print Success only when the status code is 200.
steps.hint: Use if status_code == 200: and indent the print statement.

payload = {'company': 'BioNova'}

# Read results with a default value of 0
results =

lessons.title: Automate repetitive data tasks
lessons.description: Process multiple files using functions and loops.

steps.title: Process several filenames
steps.content:
Automation often means applying the same rule to many inputs.

Loop through the filenames and print only CSV files.
steps.starter:
files = ['sales.csv', 'notes.txt', 'customers.csv', 'image.png']

# Print only filenames ending in .csv
steps.hint: Loop through files and use filename.endswith('.csv').

steps.title: Create a reusable cleaner
steps.content: Functions make automation easier to test and reuse.
steps.starter:
def clean_filename(filename):
    # Remove surrounding spaces and convert to lowercase
    pass

print(clean_filename('  SALES JULY.CSV  '))

steps.title: Build an output list
steps.content: List comprehensions can express short transformations clearly.
steps.starter:
files = [' A.CSV ', ' B.csv ', 'notes.txt']
# Create cleaned_csvs containing cleaned CSV filenames only
steps.hint: Filter with endswith after strip/lower, and transform using strip().lower().
steps.solution:
cleaned_csvs = [f.strip().lower() for f in files if f.strip().lower().endswith('.csv')]
print(cleaned_csvs)

modules.title: Module 6 · Advanced Python
modules.description: Understand generators, decorators, context managers, type hints and asynchronous workflows.

lessons.title: Generators and lazy iteration
lessons.description: Produce values one at a time instead of storing everything in memory.

steps.title: Yield values gradually
steps.content:
A generator uses yield to produce one value at a time.

Create count_up_to(limit) and yield numbers from 1 to the limit.
steps.starter:
def count_up_to(limit):
    # Yield numbers from 1 to limit
    pass

print(list(count_up_to(4)))
steps.hint: Use a for loop with range(1, limit + 1), then yield each number.

steps.title: Filter with a generator
steps.content: Generators can combine filtering and lazy iteration.
steps.starter:
def even_numbers(values):
    # Yield only even values
    pass

print(list(even_numbers([1,2,3,4,5,6])))
steps.hint: Loop through values and yield a value when value % 2 == 0.

steps.title: Understand lazy evaluation
steps.content: Recall why generators are useful.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: They do not need to create every value in advance.

quiz.question: What is a major benefit of generators?
options: They produce values lazily and can reduce memory use
They automatically use multiple CPU cores

lessons.title: Decorators and reusable behaviour
lessons.description: Wrap functions to add logging, timing or validation.

steps.title: Wrap a function
steps.content: A decorator receives a function and returns a new function.
steps.hint: Inside wrapper, print 'Starting' and then call func().

steps.title: Return the wrapped value
steps.content: A wrapper should often return the original function result.

steps.title: Identify decorator syntax
steps.content: Recall how decorators are applied.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: It appears above the function definition.

quiz.question: Which symbol is used to apply a decorator?

lessons.title: Context managers
lessons.description: Manage files and resources safely with with-blocks.

steps.title: Use a with-block
steps.content: A context manager handles setup and cleanup automatically.
steps.starter:
from io import StringIO
stream = StringIO('PythonQuest')
# Use with stream as resource and print resource.read()

steps.title: Create a custom context manager
steps.content: The contextmanager decorator can create simple context managers.
steps.starter:
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def managed_message():
    print('Open')
    yield
    print('Close')

with managed_message():
    print('Working')
steps.hint: Run it and observe the order.

steps.title: Understand cleanup
steps.content: Recall why context managers are useful.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: Think about files and database connections.

quiz.question: What does a context manager primarily help with?

lessons.title: Type hints and readable contracts
lessons.description: Describe expected inputs and outputs without changing runtime behaviour.

steps.title: Annotate a function
steps.content: Type hints make intent clearer for readers and tools.

steps.title: Use optional values
steps.content: A value may be a string or None.
steps.hint: Annotate name as str | None and the return as str.

steps.title: Know what hints do
steps.content: Python remains dynamically typed.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: They describe intent rather than enforcing it automatically.

quiz.question: Do standard Python type hints automatically block every incorrect type at runtime?
options: No, they mainly support readability and tooling

lessons.title: Async Python fundamentals
lessons.description: Understand coroutines, await and concurrent waiting.

steps.title: Create a coroutine
steps.content: async def defines a coroutine. await pauses it without blocking other async work.
steps.starter:
import asyncio

async def fetch_value():
    await asyncio.sleep(0)
    return 42

# Run the coroutine and print the result

steps.title: Run tasks together
steps.content: asyncio.gather waits for several coroutines together.
steps.starter:
import asyncio

async def get_number(value):
    await asyncio.sleep(0)
    return value

# Gather three calls
steps.solution:
results = await asyncio.gather(get_number(1), get_number(2), get_number(3))
print(results)

steps.title: Understand async use cases
steps.content: Async is useful for waiting-heavy workloads.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: Which workload is often a good fit for async Python?

modules.title: Module 7 · Testing and Debugging
modules.description: Write reliable Python using assertions, unit tests, exceptions and systematic debugging.

lessons.title: Assertions and expected behaviour
lessons.description: Turn assumptions into executable checks.

steps.title: Check a function result
steps.content:
An assertion states what must be true. If the condition is false, Python raises an AssertionError.

Add an assertion verifying that add(2, 3) equals 5.
steps.starter:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

# Add an assertion below
print('Check completed')

steps.title: Add a helpful message
steps.content: An assertion message explains what failed.
steps.solution:
assert calculate_discount(100, 0.2) == 80, 'Discount calculation failed'
print('Discount check passed')

steps.title: Know what assertions are for
steps.content: Assertions are especially useful during development and testing.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: What happens when an assertion condition is false?

lessons.title: Unit tests with unittest
lessons.description: Organise repeatable tests for functions and edge cases.

steps.title: Create a test case
steps.content: Python's built-in unittest module groups related tests.

steps.title: Test an edge case
steps.content: Good tests include boundaries and unusual inputs.

steps.title: Understand unit-test scope
steps.content: A unit test normally focuses on one small behaviour.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: What should a unit test usually verify?

lessons.title: Exceptions and defensive code
lessons.description: Handle expected failures without hiding every error.

steps.title: Catch a specific exception
steps.content:
Catch only the errors you expect and know how to handle.

Catch ValueError when converting invalid text to an integer.
steps.hint: Replace the bare except with except ValueError:.

steps.title: Raise a meaningful error
steps.content: Functions should reject invalid input clearly.
steps.hint: Use if not values: raise ValueError('values cannot be empty').

steps.title: Avoid over-catching
steps.content: A broad exception handler can hide unrelated defects.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: Catch the narrowest exception you can handle.

quiz.question: Why is a bare except often discouraged?

lessons.title: A systematic debugging workflow
lessons.description: Reproduce, isolate, inspect and verify defects.

steps.title: Fix an accumulating total
steps.content: The total is reset inside the loop, so only the final value survives.
steps.starter:
sales = [100, 200, 300]
for amount in sales:
    total = 0
    total += amount

print(total)
steps.solution:
sales = [100, 200, 300]
total = 0
for amount in sales:
    total += amount

print(total)

steps.title: Fix a mutable default
steps.content: A mutable default list is shared between function calls.
steps.starter:
def add_item(item, items=[]):
    items.append(item)
    return items

first = add_item('A')
second = add_item('B')
print(first)
print(second)
steps.hint: Use items=None, then create a new list inside the function.
steps.solution:
def add_item(item, items=None):
    if items is None:
        items = []
    items.append(item)
    return items

first = add_item('A')
second = add_item('B')
print(first)
print(second)

modules.title: Module 8 · Data Engineering Foundations
modules.description: Build repeatable ETL pipelines with validation, logging and incremental processing.

lessons.title: Design an ETL pipeline
lessons.description: Separate extraction, transformation and loading into testable stages.

steps.title: Separate pipeline stages
steps.content:
ETL stands for extract, transform and load. Keeping each stage separate makes the workflow easier to test and reuse.

Complete the transform stage so it adds a net_revenue field.
steps.hint: Loop through records, copy each dictionary and add revenue - cost.
steps.solution:
def transform(records):
    transformed = []
    for record in records:
        clean = record.copy()
        clean['net_revenue'] = clean['revenue'] - clean['cost']
        transformed.append(clean)
    return transformed

steps.title: Compose the pipeline
steps.content: A pipeline function coordinates the stages without mixing their responsibilities.
steps.starter:
def run_pipeline():
    # Extract, transform, load and return the result
    pass

print(run_pipeline())

steps.title: Understand pipeline separation
steps.content: Recall why stages are separated.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: Why separate extract, transform and load stages?
options: To improve testing, reuse and maintainability

lessons.title: Validate schemas and data quality
lessons.description: Reject malformed records before they reach downstream analysis.

steps.title: Check required fields
steps.content: Schema validation confirms that required fields are present before processing continues.

steps.title: Validate field types
steps.content: Type checks catch values that would break calculations.
steps.starter:
record = {'order_id': 101, 'revenue': 250, 'region': 'North'}

# Create type_valid checking order_id is int, revenue is numeric and region is str
steps.hint: Use isinstance and allow int or float for revenue.
steps.solution:
type_valid = (
    isinstance(record['order_id'], int)
    and isinstance(record['revenue'], (int, float))
    and isinstance(record['region'], str)
)
print(type_valid)

steps.title: Return useful validation errors
steps.content: A Boolean says that something failed. A list of errors explains what failed.

steps.content: Useful logs include measurable context.

steps.content: Different levels communicate different severity.

quiz.question: Which level suits a recoverable issue that deserves attention?

lessons.title: Process data incrementally
lessons.description: Handle only new records instead of rebuilding everything.

steps.title: Filter new records
steps.content: Incremental processing uses a checkpoint such as the highest processed ID or timestamp.
steps.starter:
records = [
    {'id': 101, 'amount': 10},
    {'id': 102, 'amount': 20},
    {'id': 103, 'amount': 30}
]
last_processed_id = 101

# Create new_records containing only records after the checkpoint
steps.solution:
new_records = [record for record in records if record['id'] > last_processed_id]
print(new_records)

steps.title: Advance the checkpoint
steps.content: The checkpoint should advance only after successful processing.
steps.starter: # Set new_checkpoint to the highest id in new_records
steps.hint: Use max(record['id'] for record in new_records).
steps.solution:
new_checkpoint = max(record['id'] for record in new_records)
print(new_checkpoint)

steps.title: Understand incremental benefits
steps.content: Recall why checkpoints matter.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: What is the main benefit of incremental processing?
options: It always removes the need for backups
It processes only new or changed data

modules.title: Module 9 · Machine Learning Foundations
modules.description: Prepare features, train simple models, evaluate predictions and think responsibly about bias.

lessons.title: Frame a machine-learning problem
lessons.description: Distinguish prediction targets, features and success metrics.

steps.title: Identify target and features
steps.content:
A supervised model learns to predict a target from one or more features.

Create the target and feature names for a churn prediction problem.

columns = ['tenure_months', 'monthly_spend', 'support_tickets', 'churned']

# Choose a metric name for a balanced binary classification problem
metric =

steps.title: Recognise leakage
steps.content: Data leakage happens when training data contains information unavailable at prediction time.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: Think about information recorded after the outcome.

quiz.question: Which feature would cause leakage when predicting whether a loan will default?

# Use the first 8 records for training and the final 2 for testing

print(train_records)
print(test_records)

steps.title: Understand overfitting
steps.content: Overfitting means learning the training data too specifically.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: Which pattern most strongly suggests overfitting?
options: Low training score and low test score
High training score and much lower test score

lessons.title: Build a simple regression model
lessons.description: Fit a line and calculate prediction error.

steps.title: Fit a line with NumPy
steps.content: Linear regression estimates a relationship between an input and a numeric target.
steps.starter:
import numpy as np

x = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], dtype=float)
y = np.array([3, 5, 7, 9, 11], dtype=float)

# Fit a first-degree polynomial and store slope and intercept
steps.solution:
slope, intercept = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)
print(round(slope, 2), round(intercept, 2))

steps.title: Calculate mean absolute error
steps.content: Mean absolute error measures the average absolute difference between prediction and reality.
steps.starter:
predictions = slope * x + intercept
# Calculate mae from y and predictions

steps.title: Interpret MAE
steps.content: MAE is expressed in the same unit as the target.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: Lower average error is better when comparing like-for-like models.

quiz.question: What does an MAE of 5 mean?
options: Predictions are wrong by 5 units on average

lessons.title: Evaluate a binary classifier
lessons.description: Calculate accuracy, precision and recall from predictions.

steps.title: Calculate accuracy
steps.content: Accuracy is the proportion of predictions that are correct.
steps.starter:
actual = [1, 0, 1, 1, 0]
predicted = [1, 0, 0, 1, 0]

# Calculate accuracy
steps.hint: Count matching pairs and divide by len(actual).
steps.solution:
correct = sum(a == p for a, p in zip(actual, predicted))
accuracy = correct / len(actual)
print(accuracy)

steps.title: Calculate precision and recall
steps.content: Precision asks how many predicted positives were correct. Recall asks how many actual positives were found.
steps.starter:
tp = sum(a == 1 and p == 1 for a, p in zip(actual, predicted))
fp = sum(a == 0 and p == 1 for a, p in zip(actual, predicted))
fn = sum(a == 1 and p == 0 for a, p in zip(actual, predicted))

# Calculate precision and recall
steps.hint: precision = tp / (tp + fp), recall = tp / (tp + fn).
steps.solution:
precision = tp / (tp + fp) if tp + fp else 0
recall = tp / (tp + fn) if tp + fn else 0
print(precision)
print(recall)

steps.title: Choose recall
steps.content: Recall matters when missing a positive case is especially costly.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')

quiz.question: When is recall often especially important?

lessons.title: Responsible model evaluation
lessons.description: Check fairness, explainability, privacy and monitoring needs.

steps.title: Compare group outcomes
steps.content: A single overall score can hide weaker performance for particular groups.
steps.starter:
group_scores = {'Group A': 0.88, 'Group B': 0.72}

# Calculate the absolute performance gap
steps.solution:
performance_gap = abs(group_scores['Group A'] - group_scores['Group B'])
print(round(performance_gap, 2))

steps.title: Create a monitoring checklist
steps.content: Models can degrade as data and behaviour change.
steps.starter: # Create checks containing accuracy, data drift and group performance
steps.hint: Use a list with those three strings.

steps.title: Recognise responsible deployment
steps.content: Human oversight and clear accountability remain important.
steps.starter: print('Answer below.')
steps.hint: A model should not be treated as infallible.

quiz.question: Which practice best supports responsible deployment?
